Greek-Entry.English-Entry: show the outward signs of, manhood
to be in the prime of youth
to have the outward signs of puberty
a place where young people meet
ἦμαρ: καθὸ ἀπαντῶσιν εἰς ταὐτόν, ἢ εὔκαιρον, ἱερόν
to be in the bloom of youth
tenure of office of a governor,
for a leader, ready to lead
the authoritative part of the soul
belonging to the prefect of Egypt
one who does a thing first, shows the way
mass of dried figs, borne in procession at the Attic
which was considered the first step in civilized life
that is to say, or rather
only then, then and not before
something that makes drink taste pleasant
ghosts of those who die unmarried,
it being well for them at their age to die
the upper part of the mast
philosophers of the school of Elis
an alloy of gold and silver
those of the same age, comrades
like the sun, bright and beaming
a room exposed to the sun
sun-mad, mad for love of the sun
live in the sun, be exposed to the sun
to be illuminated by the sunlight
green stone streaked with red, blood-stone
missing the work, failing in one"s aim,
a plant useful to staunch blood
that which is thrown, dart, javelin
in a day, within a day"s space
the needs of the day, one"s daily bread
on one day, and on the next . .
one who lends on daily interest
for the hunting of tame beasts
in the form of a diary
a woman"s dress folded at the top so as to fall half-way down the figure
Mémoires présentés à l" Acad. des Inscr.
forming half a pair of scales
pain on one side of the head
multiplying by one and a half
containing one and a half, half as much
in the ratio of one and a half to one
a light vessel with one and a half banks of oars
consisting of a metre and a half
half the apparent breadth of the moon
in which the prize is half a talent
one of the two frames containing the
the left being the bridle hand
one who holds the reins, driver, charioteer
not to be looked at, dazzling
for the sake of, on account of, for
to be a follower of Heraclitus
to be still, keep quiet, be at rest
at which they wore spring flowers
ἦρον ὠνουμένων ἔδωκεν . . , τὸν ἦρον ἐνδήσαντι . . , τέλος ἐν Πάρῳ τοῦ ἤρου
τὰ Θεοδαίσια: οἱ δὲ ἑορτήν, οἱ δὲ ἱερά
poem consisting of hexameters and iambics
to be cast in a suit
give way, be a slave to
the seat of feeling, passion, desire
suffer from noises in the ears
at dawn, at break of day
